verses.commentary: Jesus travels from city to village, preaching and bringing good news of the kingdom of God. The Twelve accompany. Itinerant ministry settled into rhythm.
Some women cured of evil spirits and infirmities also travel — Mary called Magdalene, from whom seven demons had gone out. Lukan distinctive: named female disciples.

Joanna (wife of Chuza, Herod's household manager — connection to royal court), Susanna, many others. They provided for him out of their means (diekonoun, διηκόνουν). Women financially sustaining the ministry.
Great crowd from town after town. He told a parable.
Sower goes out to sow. Some seed fell along the path — trampled, birds devoured. First soil category.
Some fell on rock — withered for lack of moisture. Shallow root, quick failure.
Some fell among thorns — thorns grew up and choked. Coexistence with rival demands.
Some fell into good soil — yielded a hundredfold. 'Whoever has ears, let him hear.' Lukan version omits the 30 and 60 — emphasizes the maximal yield.
Disciples ask what the parable means. Private interpretation requested.

'To you it has been given to know the secrets (mysteria, μυστήρια) of the kingdom; for others they are in parables, so that seeing they may not see, and hearing they may not understand.' Isaiah 6:9 again.
Interpretation: 'The seed is the word of God.' Logos tou theou — explicit Lukan formulation.
Path = those who hear; devil comes and takes away the word from their heart, lest they believe and be saved. Soteriology embedded — believing leads to being saved.
Rock = receive with joy; have no root; believe for a while; in time of testing (peirasmou, πειρασμοῦ) fall away. Joy-receivers fail under trial.
Thorns = hear, but as they go their way they are choked by cares, riches, pleasures of life; fruit does not mature. Three thorns named: anxiety, wealth, hedonism.

Good soil = hearing the word with a noble and good heart (kale kai agathe, καλῇ καὶ ἀγαθῇ), hold it fast, bear fruit with endurance (hypomone, ὑπομονή). The fourth soil's signature is patient holding.
No one lights a lamp to cover with a jar or under a bed — puts on a stand so those who enter may see the light. Hidden light betrays its purpose.
Nothing hidden that will not be made manifest; secret thing that will not be known. Disclosure is eschatological certainty.
Take care how you hear. Whoever has, more will be given; whoever has not, even what he thinks he has will be taken away. The 'thinks he has' is unique to Luke — diagnostic of self-deception.
Mother and brothers come, cannot reach him for the crowd. Family proximity blocked.
'Your mother and brothers stand outside, wanting to see you.' Message relayed.
'My mother and my brothers are those who hear the word of God and do it.' Kinship redefined by hearing-and-doing. The seed parable's good soil takes flesh in new family.
One day Jesus gets into a boat with disciples: 'Let us go across to the other side of the lake.' They set out.
As they sailed, he fell asleep. A windstorm came down on the lake; they were filling with water, in danger. The unbothered sovereign sleeps amid chaos.
Disciples wake him: 'Master, Master, we are perishing!' He awoke, rebuked wind and raging waters; they ceased; calm. The same rebuke-verb as for demons.
'Where is your faith?' Afraid and marveling: 'Who then is this, that he commands even winds and water, and they obey him?' Christological question forced.
They sailed to the country of the Gerasenes, opposite Galilee. Gentile territory.
Stepping ashore — meets a man from the city who had demons. For long time wore no clothes, did not live in a house, lived among tombs. Total social-spatial disintegration.
Seeing Jesus, cried out, fell down before him: 'What have you to do with me, Jesus, Son of the Most High God? I beg you, do not torment me.' Demonic Christology recognizes Jesus' rank.
Jesus had commanded the unclean spirit to come out — for many times it had seized him; kept under guard, chains, broken away, driven by the demon into the desert.
'What is your name?' 'Legion' — for many demons had entered him. Legion = Roman military unit of ~6000. Mass possession.
They beg him not to command them to depart into the abyss (abysson, ἄβυσσον — primordial deep, prison for demons in Jewish apocalyptic). They have eschatological fear.
Large herd of pigs feeding on the hillside. Demons beg permission to enter them. He gave them leave.
Demons go from the man into the pigs; the herd rushed down the steep bank into the lake and drowned. The unclean returns to chaos. The man is freed at the cost of one herd.
Herdsmen flee, tell in city and countryside. Economic event reported.
People come to see — find the man from whom demons had gone, sitting at Jesus' feet, clothed, in his right mind (sophronounta, σωφρονοῦντα). They are afraid. Restored sanity is also frightening.
Those who saw it told how he had been healed (esothe, ἐσώθη — saved). The verb 'saved' carries both senses.
All the people of the country of the Gerasenes asked him to depart — for they were seized with great fear. Demon-liberation is rejected when the economic cost is reckoned.
The freed man begs to go with Jesus. The exorcist sends him back.

'Return to your home and declare how much God has done for you.' The man goes throughout the whole city telling how much Jesus had done. God's act = Jesus' act. Mission to Gentiles begins with this single man.
Returning, the crowd welcomed him — all waiting for him. Galilee receives back the one Gerasa rejected.
Jairus, ruler of the synagogue, falls at Jesus' feet, implores him to come to his house.
His only daughter (Lukan precision — Luke notes 'only child' three times: 7:12, 8:42, 9:38), about twelve years old, was dying. Crowds press around as he goes.

A woman who had had a discharge of blood twelve years — twelve years of impurity, contrasting Jairus' daughter's twelve years of life. (Many manuscripts add 'and could not be healed by anyone' — possibly Luke the physician softening Mark's 'spent all she had.')
She came up behind, touched the fringe (kraspedon, κράσπεδον) of his garment. Immediately the flow of blood ceased.
'Who was it that touched me?' All deny. Peter (in some manuscripts): 'Master, the crowds surround and press in on you.'
'Someone touched me — I perceived that power has gone out from me.' Power flows by touch initiated by faith.
Seeing she was not hidden, the woman came trembling, fell down before him, declared in the presence of all the people why she had touched him and how she was immediately healed. Public confession.
'Daughter, your faith has made you well (sesoken se, σέσωκέν σε); go in peace.' Faith as the salvific medium.
While he was still speaking — someone from Jairus' house: 'Your daughter is dead. Trouble the teacher no further.' Bad news arrives mid-stream.
Jesus hears: 'Do not fear; only believe, and she will be well (sothesetai, σωθήσεται).' Faith-call before the rescue.
At the house — allows only Peter, John, James, the father and mother. The inner circle plus parents. Witness restricted.
All weeping and mourning. 'Do not weep; she is not dead but sleeping.' Sleep as euphemism for death anticipating resurrection.
They laughed at him, knowing she was dead. Mockery before miracle.
Taking her hand: 'Child, arise.' Simple word.
Her spirit returned; she rose immediately. He directed that something be given her to eat. Practical care after resurrection — confirms bodily reality.
Parents amazed. He charged them to tell no one what had happened. Messianic restraint persists even after raising the dead.
